---
format:
    revealjs:
        theme: ../../styles/meds-slides-styles.scss
        slide-number: true
        chalkboard: true
        title-slide: false
jupyter: eds232-env
---

```{python}
#| echo: false
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import pandas as pd
from sklearn.linear_model import LinearRegression
import statsmodels.api as sm

plt.style.use('default')
fig_size_x = 8
fig_size_y = 4
plt.rcParams['font.size'] = 10

np.random.seed(7)
n = 200

process    = np.random.uniform(0, 300, n)
efficiency = np.random.uniform(0,  50, n)
offsets    = np.random.uniform(0, 100, n)

co2_reduction = (2.939
                 + 0.046  * process
                 + 0.189  * efficiency
                 - 0.001  * offsets
                 + np.random.normal(0, 1.69, n))

df = pd.DataFrame({
    'process':       process,
    'efficiency':    efficiency,
    'offsets':       offsets,
    'co2_reduction': co2_reduction
})

# Fit models
model_proc = LinearRegression().fit(df[['process']], df['co2_reduction'])
b0 = model_proc.intercept_
b1 = model_proc.coef_[0]
x_line = np.linspace(0, 300, 300)

X_ols = sm.add_constant(df['process'])
res   = sm.OLS(df['co2_reduction'], X_ols).fit()

ci = res.conf_int(alpha=0.05)
ci_b1_low  = ci.loc['process', 0]
ci_b1_high = ci.loc['process', 1]

y_hat = model_proc.predict(df[['process']])
y_bar = df['co2_reduction'].mean()
TSS   = np.sum((df['co2_reduction'] - y_bar)**2)
RSS   = np.sum((df['co2_reduction'] - y_hat)**2)
R2    = 1 - RSS / TSS
RSE   = np.sqrt(RSS / (n - 2))
```

## {#title-slide data-menu-title="Title Slide" background="#053660"}

[EDS 232]{.custom-title}

<hr class="hr-teal">

[Lesson 3]{.custom-subtitle}

[*Simple linear regression*]{.custom-subtitle}

---

## {#in-this-lesson data-menu-title="In this lesson"}

[In this lesson]{.slide-title}

<hr>

<br>

- The simple linear regression (SLR) model
- Estimating coefficients with least squares
- Standard errors and confidence intervals
- Hypothesis testing: $t$-statistic and $p$-values
- Assessing model accuracy: RSE and $R^2$

---

## {#section-example data-menu-title="# A guiding example #" background="#047C90"}

<div class="page-center vertical-center">
<p class="custom-subtitle bottombr">A guiding example</p>
</div>

---

## {#greenabate data-menu-title="The GreenAbate dataset"}

[The GreenAbate dataset]{.slide-title}

<hr>

::: {.body-text-m}
A research consortium tracks **200 manufacturing firms**. For each firm, they record annual spending on three emissions-reduction strategies and the resulting CO₂ savings:
:::

<br>

::: {.body-text-m}
- **`process`** — investment in cleaner production processes (\$K/year)
- **`efficiency`** — energy efficiency upgrades (\$K/year)
- **`offsets`** — carbon offset purchases (\$K/year)
- **`co2_reduction`** — annual CO₂ emissions reduced (ktCO₂e/year)
:::

<br>

::: {.center-text .body-text-m .teal-text}
*Do these investments actually drive emissions reductions, and if so, by how much?*
:::

---

## {#greenabate-plots data-menu-title="GreenAbate: data overview"}

[CO₂ reduction vs. each abatement channel]{.slide-title}

<hr>

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| fig-align: center
#| out-width: "100%"

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 3, figsize=(fig_size_x * 1.5, fig_size_y))

specs = [
    ('process',    'steelblue',  'Process upgrades ($K)'),
    ('efficiency', 'darkorange', 'Efficiency upgrades ($K)'),
    ('offsets',    'seagreen',   'Carbon offsets ($K)'),
]

for ax, (col, color, xlabel) in zip(axes, specs):
    ax.scatter(df[col], df['co2_reduction'], s=12, alpha=0.5, color=color)
    ax.set_xlabel(xlabel)
    ax.set_ylabel('CO₂ reduction (ktCO₂e)')
    ax.grid(True, alpha=0.3)

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
plt.close()
```

::: {.center-text .body-text-s}
*Synthetic data generated for educational purposes only*
:::

---

## {#section-slr data-menu-title="# Simple linear regression #" background="#047C90"}

<div class="page-center vertical-center">
<p class="custom-subtitle bottombr">Simple linear regression</p>
</div>

---

## {#slr-model data-menu-title="The SLR model"}

[The simple linear regression model]{.slide-title}

<hr>

**Simple linear regression (SLR)** assumes a linear relationship between a single predictor $X$ and a response $Y$:

$$Y \approx \beta_0 + \beta_1 X$$

. . .

::: {.body-text-m}
- $\beta_0$ — **intercept**: expected value of $Y$ when $X = 0$
- $\beta_1$ — **slope**: average change in $Y$ for a one-unit increase in $X$
:::

. . .

<br>

Since $\beta_0$ and $\beta_1$ are unknown, we **estimate** them from data:

$$\hat{y} = \hat{\beta}_0 + \hat{\beta}_1 x = \hat{f}(x)$$

::: {.body-text-s .teal-text .center-text}
The $\hat{\cdot}$ notation always denotes an *estimated* quantity.
:::

---

## {#slr-greenabate data-menu-title="SLR: fitted line"}

[Fitting SLR to the GreenAbate data]{.slide-title}

<hr>

Using `process` as the sole predictor:

$$\texttt{co2\_reduction} \approx \hat{\beta}_0 + \hat{\beta}_1 \cdot \texttt{process}$$

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| fig-align: center
#| out-width: "100%"

plt.figure(figsize=(fig_size_x, fig_size_y - 0.5))
plt.scatter(df['process'], df['co2_reduction'], s=15, alpha=0.5, color='steelblue', label='Observed data')
plt.plot(x_line, b0 + b1 * x_line, 'b-', linewidth=2.5,
         label=f'$\\hat{{y}} = {b0:.2f} + {b1:.4f}\\,x$')
plt.xlabel('Process upgrade spending ($K)')
plt.ylabel('CO₂ reduction (ktCO₂e)')
plt.grid(True, alpha=0.3)
plt.legend(loc='upper left')
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
plt.close()
```

---

## {#checkin-slr data-menu-title="Check-in: coefficients" background="#053660"}

<div class="page-center vertical-center">
<p class="custom-subtitle bottombr">Check-in</p>
<p class="custom-subtitle3">In the context of the GreenAbate problem:</p>
<p class="custom-subtitle3">What does $\hat{\beta}_0$ represent? What does $\hat{\beta}_1$ represent?</p>
<p class="custom-subtitle3">If a firm spends \$150K on process upgrades, what is the predicted CO₂ reduction?</p>
</div>

---

## {#section-estimation data-menu-title="# Estimating the coefficients #" background="#047C90"}

<div class="page-center vertical-center">
<p class="custom-subtitle bottombr">Estimating the coefficients</p>
</div>

---

## {#residuals-def data-menu-title="Residuals and RSS"}

[Residuals and the RSS]{.slide-title}

<hr>

The **residual** for observation $i$ is the difference between observed and predicted:

$$e_i = y_i - \hat{y}_i = y_i - \hat{\beta}_0 - \hat{\beta}_1 x_i$$

. . .

The **residual sum of squares (RSS)** totals all squared residuals:

$$RSS = \sum_{i=1}^{n} e_i^2 = \sum_{i=1}^{n}(y_i - \hat{\beta}_0 - \hat{\beta}_1 x_i)^2$$

. . .

<br>

::: {.center-text .body-text-m .teal-text}
The **least squares** approach chooses $\hat{\beta}_0$ and $\hat{\beta}_1$ to **minimize** the RSS.
:::

---

## {#residuals-plot data-menu-title="Residuals: visual"}

[Residuals as vertical distances to the fitted line]{.slide-title}

<hr>

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| fig-align: center
#| out-width: "100%"

plt.figure(figsize=(fig_size_x, fig_size_y))
plt.scatter(df['process'], df['co2_reduction'], s=12, alpha=0.4, color='steelblue', zorder=2, label='Observed data')
plt.plot(x_line, b0 + b1 * x_line, 'b-', linewidth=2.5, zorder=3, label='Fitted line')

for i, (xi, yi) in enumerate(zip(df['process'], df['co2_reduction'])):
    yi_hat = b0 + b1 * xi
    lbl = 'Residual $e_i$' if i == 0 else None
    plt.plot([xi, xi], [yi, yi_hat], color='tomato', linewidth=0.6, alpha=0.6, label=lbl, zorder=1)

plt.xlabel('Process upgrade spending ($K)')
plt.ylabel('CO₂ reduction (ktCO₂e)')
plt.grid(True, alpha=0.3)
plt.legend(loc='upper left')
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
plt.close()
```

---

## {#ls-formulas data-menu-title="Least squares formulas"}

[Least squares solution]{.slide-title}

<hr>

Minimizing the RSS gives closed-form expressions for the coefficients:

$$\hat{\beta}_1 = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_i - \bar{x})(y_i - \bar{y})}{\sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_i - \bar{x})^2}$$

$$\hat{\beta}_0 = \bar{y} - \hat{\beta}_1 \bar{x}$$

. . .

<br>

::: {.body-text-m}
Two important points:

1. The estimates depend entirely on the training data — different data, different estimates. This is the source of estimation uncertainty.

2. In practice, **the computer computes these** for you.
:::

---

## {#section-accuracy data-menu-title="# Coefficient accuracy #" background="#047C90"}

<div class="page-center vertical-center">
<p class="custom-subtitle bottombr">Accuracy of the coefficient estimates</p>
</div>

---

## {#standard-errors data-menu-title="Standard errors"}

[Standard errors]{.slide-title}

<hr>

::: {.body-text-m}
The true data-generating process is $Y = \beta_0 + \beta_1 X + \epsilon$. The **standard error** quantifies how far $\hat{\beta}$ is from the true $\beta$ on average.
:::

$$SE(\hat{\beta}_1)^2 = \frac{\sigma^2}{\sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_i - \bar{x})^2}$$

$$SE(\hat{\beta}_0)^2 = \sigma^2 \left[\frac{1}{n} + \frac{\bar{x}^2}{\sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_i - \bar{x})^2}\right]$$

. . .

::: {.body-text-s}
where $\sigma^2 = \text{Var}(\epsilon)$ is the error variance (unknown in practice — estimated from the data).
:::

---

## {#checkin-se data-menu-title="Check-in: standard errors" background="#053660"}

<div class="page-center vertical-center">
<p class="custom-subtitle bottombr">Check-in</p>
<p class="custom-subtitle3">How does $SE(\hat{\beta}_1)$ change as the $x$ values become more spread out?</p>
<p class="custom-subtitle3">Does this make intuitive sense?</p>
</div>

---

## {#confidence-intervals data-menu-title="Confidence intervals"}

[Confidence intervals]{.slide-title}

<hr>

A **95% confidence interval** for a coefficient $\beta_i$ is:

$$[\hat{\beta}_i - 2 \cdot SE(\hat{\beta}_i),\;\; \hat{\beta}_i + 2 \cdot SE(\hat{\beta}_i)]$$

::: {.body-text-s}
*(the factor 2 is approximate; the exact value comes from the $t$-distribution with $n-2$ d.f.)*
:::

. . .

<br>

::: {.body-text-m}
**Interpretation:**

> A 95% CI for $\beta_1$ of $[a_1, b_1]$ means there is a 95% chance that a one-unit increase in $x$ is associated with an average increase in $y$ between $a_1$ and $b_1$ units.

> A 95% CI for $\beta_0$ of $[a_0, b_0]$ means there is a 95% chance that the regression line crosses the $y$-axis between $a_0$ and $b_0$.
:::

---

## {#ci-greenabate data-menu-title="CIs: GreenAbate"}

[95% confidence intervals for the GreenAbate SLR]{.slide-title}

<hr>

```{python}
#| echo: false
ci_df = pd.DataFrame({
    '':       ['Intercept', 'process'],
    '2.5%':   [f"{ci.loc['const', 0]:.4f}",   f"{ci.loc['process', 0]:.4f}"],
    '97.5%':  [f"{ci.loc['const', 1]:.4f}",   f"{ci.loc['process', 1]:.4f}"],
})
print(ci_df.to_string(index=False))
```

. . .

<br>

::: {.body-text-m}
- The 95% CI for $\beta_1$ (process) is $[`{python} f"{ci_b1_low:.3f}"`,\; `{python} f"{ci_b1_high:.3f}"`]$ — entirely above 0.
- Strong evidence that higher process spending is associated with greater CO₂ reductions.
:::

---

## {#checkin-ci data-menu-title="Check-in: confidence intervals" background="#053660"}

<div class="page-center vertical-center">
<p class="custom-subtitle bottombr">Check-in</p>
<p class="custom-subtitle3">The 95% CI for $\beta_1$ is $[`{python} f"{ci_b1_low:.3f}"`,\; `{python} f"{ci_b1_high:.3f}"`]$.</p>
<p class="custom-subtitle3">What if the interval had been $[-0.002,\; `{python} f"{ci_b1_high:.3f}"`]$ instead?</p>
<p class="custom-subtitle3">What would that tell us about the relationship?</p>
</div>

---

## {#section-hypothesis data-menu-title="# Hypothesis testing #" background="#047C90"}

<div class="page-center vertical-center">
<p class="custom-subtitle bottombr">Hypothesis testing</p>
</div>

---

## {#hypotheses data-menu-title="Null and alternative hypotheses"}

[Is there a relationship between $X$ and $Y$?]{.slide-title}

<hr>

<br>

::: {.body-text-m}
- **Null hypothesis** $H_0$: $\beta_1 = 0$ — no relationship between $X$ and $Y$

  → $\hat{Y} = \hat{\beta}_0$ (a constant; $X$ carries no information)

- **Alternative hypothesis** $H_a$: $\beta_1 \neq 0$ — there is a relationship

  → $\hat{Y} = \hat{\beta}_0 + \hat{\beta}_1 X$
:::

. . .

<br>

::: {.center-text .body-text-m .teal-text}
To test $H_0$, we need to determine whether $\hat{\beta}_1$ is sufficiently far from 0 relative to its uncertainty.
:::

---

## {#t-stat data-menu-title="t-statistic and p-value"}

[The $t$-statistic and $p$-value]{.slide-title}

<hr>

The **$t$-statistic** measures how many standard deviations $\hat{\beta}_1$ is from 0:

$$t = \frac{\hat{\beta}_1}{SE(\hat{\beta}_1)}$$

. . .

The **$p$-value** is the probability of observing $|t|$ this large, *assuming $H_0$ is true*.

. . .

<br>

::: {.body-text-m}
- **Small $p$-value** ($p < 0.05$): unlikely to see $\hat{\beta}_1$ this far from 0 by chance → **reject $H_0$** → evidence of a relationship
- **Large $p$-value**: data are consistent with $\beta_1 = 0$ → **fail to reject $H_0$**
:::

---

## {#regression-table data-menu-title="Regression table"}

[SLR output: `co2_reduction` ~ `process`]{.slide-title}

<hr>

```{python}
#| echo: false
def fmt_p(p):
    return '< 0.0001' if p < 0.0001 else f'{p:.4f}'

coef_df = pd.DataFrame({
    '':              ['Intercept', 'process'],
    'Coefficient':   [f"{res.params['const']:.4f}",   f"{res.params['process']:.4f}"],
    'Std. error':    [f"{res.bse['const']:.4f}",       f"{res.bse['process']:.4f}"],
    't-statistic':   [f"{res.tvalues['const']:.2f}",   f"{res.tvalues['process']:.2f}"],
    'p-value':       [fmt_p(res.pvalues['const']),      fmt_p(res.pvalues['process'])],
})
print(coef_df.to_string(index=False))
```

. . .

<br>

::: {.body-text-m}
Each additional \$1K in process upgrade spending is associated with **`{python} f"{b1:.4f}"` ktCO₂e** (~`{python} f"{b1*1000:.0f}"` metric tons) of additional annual CO₂ reduction.
:::

---

## {#checkin-pvalue data-menu-title="Check-in: p-values" background="#053660"}

<div class="page-center vertical-center">
<p class="custom-subtitle bottombr">Check-in</p>
<p class="custom-subtitle3">The $p$-value for `process` is < 0.0001. What does this tell us?</p>
<p class="custom-subtitle3">The intercept also has a very small $p$-value. Does this mean the intercept is an important predictor of CO₂ reductions?</p>
</div>

---

## {#section-model-accuracy data-menu-title="# Model accuracy #" background="#047C90"}

<div class="page-center vertical-center">
<p class="custom-subtitle bottombr">Assessing model accuracy</p>
</div>

---

## {#rse data-menu-title="Residual Standard Error"}

[Residual Standard Error (RSE)]{.slide-title}

<hr>

The **RSE** estimates the standard deviation of $\epsilon$ — the average amount the response deviates from the true regression line:

$$RSE = \sqrt{\frac{1}{n-2} RSS} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{n-2} \sum_{i=1}^{n}(y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2}$$

. . .

<br>

::: {.body-text-m}
- A smaller RSE means predictions are closer to observed values
- RSE is in the **same units as $Y$** → directly interpretable
- "Small" is context-dependent: compare relative to the range or mean of $Y$
:::

---

## {#checkin-rse data-menu-title="Check-in: RSE" background="#053660"}

<div class="page-center vertical-center">
<p class="custom-subtitle bottombr">Check-in</p>
<p class="custom-subtitle3">For the GreenAbate SLR, RSE ≈ `{python} f"{RSE:.2f}"` ktCO₂e (≈ `{python} f"{RSE*1000:.0f}"` metric tons).</p>
<p class="custom-subtitle3">Is this a good or bad fit? What additional information would you need to judge this?</p>
</div>

---

## {#r2-def data-menu-title="R² statistic"}

[$R^2$ statistic]{.slide-title}

<hr>

$R^2$ measures the **proportion of variance in $Y$ explained by the model**:

$$R^2 = \frac{TSS - RSS}{TSS} = 1 - \frac{RSS}{TSS}$$

where:

- $TSS = \sum(y_i - \bar{y})^2$ — **total** variability in $Y$
- $RSS = \sum(y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2$ — variability **unexplained** by the regression

. . .

$$\underbrace{TSS}_{\text{total variability}} - \underbrace{RSS}_{\text{unexplained}} = \underbrace{TSS - RSS}_{\text{explained by regression}}$$

---

## {#r2-interpret data-menu-title="Interpreting R²"}

[Interpreting $R^2$]{.slide-title}

<hr>

<br>

::: {.body-text-m}
- $R^2 \in [0, 1]$ always

- $R^2$ close to **1** → model explains most of the variability → good fit

- $R^2$ close to **0** → $X$ alone is not capturing what drives $Y$
  - Could mean: non-linear relationship, more predictors needed, high irreducible noise

- What counts as "good" is **context-dependent**
:::

. . .

<br>

::: {.center-text .body-text-m .teal-text}
Always look at your data — a high $R^2$ does not guarantee a good model!
:::

---

## {#accuracy-table data-menu-title="Model accuracy: GreenAbate"}

[Model accuracy: `co2_reduction` ~ `process`]{.slide-title}

<hr>

```{python}
#| echo: false
acc_df = pd.DataFrame({
    'Quantity': ['Residual standard error', 'R²'],
    'Value':    [f'{RSE:.2f} ktCO₂e', f'{R2:.3f}']
})
print(acc_df.to_string(index=False))
```

```{python}
#| echo: false
#| fig-align: center
#| out-width: "100%"

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(fig_size_x, fig_size_y - 0.5))

axes[0].scatter(df['process'], df['co2_reduction'], s=12, alpha=0.4, color='steelblue')
axes[0].plot(x_line, b0 + b1 * x_line, 'b-', linewidth=2)
for xi, yi in zip(df['process'], df['co2_reduction']):
    axes[0].plot([xi, xi], [yi, b0 + b1 * xi], color='tomato', linewidth=0.5, alpha=0.5)
axes[0].set_xlabel('Process upgrade spending ($K)')
axes[0].set_ylabel('CO₂ reduction (ktCO₂e)')
axes[0].set_title(f'Fit  |  RSE = {RSE:.2f}')
axes[0].grid(True, alpha=0.3)

axes[1].scatter(y_hat, df['co2_reduction'] - y_hat, s=12, alpha=0.5, color='steelblue')
axes[1].axhline(0, color='gray', linewidth=1, linestyle='--')
axes[1].set_xlabel('Fitted values $\\hat{y}_i$')
axes[1].set_ylabel('Residuals $e_i$')
axes[1].set_title(f'Residuals vs Fitted  |  $R^2$ = {R2:.3f}')
axes[1].grid(True, alpha=0.3)

plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
plt.close()
```

---

## {#checkin-r2 data-menu-title="Check-in: R²" background="#053660"}

<div class="page-center vertical-center">
<p class="custom-subtitle bottombr">Check-in</p>
<p class="custom-subtitle3">$R^2$ = `{python} f"{R2:.3f}"` and RSE = `{python} f"{RSE:.2f}"` ktCO₂e for the SLR on `process`.</p>
<p class="custom-subtitle3">How would you interpret $R^2$ in plain language?</p>
<p class="custom-subtitle3">Is RSE = `{python} f"{RSE:.2f}"` a good fit for a firm targeting 5 ktCO₂e/year? What about 25 ktCO₂e/year?</p>
</div>
